2002-02-01.title: Russian Says Kremlin Faked 'Terror Attacks'
2002-02-01.abstract: Boris A Berezovsky, Russian oligarch, says he has documentary evidence that Russia's security services were involved in apartment-house explosions in Sept 1999 that killed more than 300 people; bombings were ascribed to Chechnya and touched off full-scale invasion of republic; Berezovsky says his investigation of bombings is reason Russia's intelligence chief Nikolai Patrushev has accused him of providing financial support to Chechen 'terrorists'; fact that charges are emerging just as Berezovsky is losing another battle to retain control of independent television channel adds to public skepticism; unsolved explosions brought terror to Russia and stand as enduring mystery of Chechen conflict; photo (M)

2002-02-01.title: In October Interview, bin Laden Hinted at Role
2002-02-01.abstract: CNN broadcasts television interview from last Oct in which Osama bin Laden says anthrax attacks last fall were 'punishment from God' and hints in vague language at having hand in Sept 11 terrorist attacks; interview, conducted by Arab television network Al Jazeera, was not shown until now; CNN says it was shown against Al Jazeera's wishes (M)

2002-02-01.title: Mexico's Supreme Court Demanding Inquiry Into '68 Massacre
2002-02-01.abstract: Mexico's Supreme Court orders government of Pres Vicente Fox to investigate 1968 massacre of student demonstrators by government troops in Mexico City; neither government nor military has ever officially acknowledged gravity of killings and no one has every been charged; official history texts used in Mexico's schools erase event; historians and human-rights activists say 300 people died and 1,000 were injured when plainclothes government snipers fired on crowd of peaceful demonstrators (M)

2002-02-01.title: U.S. Troops Begin Exercises In Philippines
2002-02-01.abstract: United States begins military training exercise with Philippine soldiers in expansion of Bush administration's war against terrorism aimed at stamping out Abu Sayyaf, Muslim extremist group in southern Philippines; more than 100 American soldiers of expected contingent of 600 are at military headquarters in Zamboanga; photo (M)

2002-02-01.title: World Briefing | Europe: Ireland: Suspected Case Of Assisted Suicide
2002-02-01.abstract: Irish police say they believe that Irish woman found dead in Dublin apartment had paid two American men more than $5,000 to help her commit suicide after meeting through Internet chat room; officials say death may be first such assisted suicide in Ireland, crime that carries 14-year prison sentence (S)

2002-02-02.title: Villagers Say Errors by U.S. Causing Grief For Innocent
2002-02-02.abstract: Zani Khel, Afghan village where twenty people died in US air strike, is example of harm to noncombatants caused by military campaign, much of which is centered in remote arid region 150 miles south of Kabul; photos; map; common theme in reports of such errors is that airstrikes and ground raids sometimes produce unwanted outcomes because they are often based on information from local Afghans settling scores among each other (M)

2002-02-02.title: 'West Wing' Withdraws Ad for Show
2002-02-02.abstract: NBC ad promoting next week's episode of The West Wingis pulled after viewers point out disturbing resemblance to plight of Daniel Pearl, Wall Street Journal reporter kidnapped in Pakistan; script written last December involves White House correspondent kidnapped in Congo (S)

2002-02-02.title: Most Recent E-Mail On Seized Reporter Sends Grim Message
2002-02-02.abstract: Fate of Wall Street Journal reporter Daniel Pearl, abducted in Pakistan, remains unknown after telephone demand for $2-million ransom is followed by e-mail message saying Pearl has been executed; message says body is in Karachi graveyard, prompting police search (M)

2002-02-02.title: World Briefing | Europe: Czech Republic: Opposition Falls Apart
2002-02-02.abstract: Czech opposition alliance dissolves in dispute over campaign debts, probably ensuring victory for governing coalition in parliamentary elections this June (S)

2002-02-02.title: Art's Message to World: Simply, Eat the Riches
2002-02-02.abstract: Diary column on World Economic Forum in New York City; Mimi Oka and Doug Fitch, whose 'art' involves provocative food presentations, prepare event for 35 people that features pasta sheets printed with $100 bills and gold-flecked meatballs(M)

2002-02-03.title: Indonesian Cleric Is Suspected of Being a Terrorist Leader
2002-02-03.abstract: FBI has said that Malaysia was staging area for Sept 11 atacks and an Al Qaeda base; Malaysian officials have strongly rejected this, but concede that Riduan Isamuddin, who was itinerant pracher in Malaysia, may have been point man for Al Qaeda in region; say he arranged for two of Sept 11 hijackers to visit Malaysia and stay in apartment belonging to one of his recruits, Yazid Sufaat, who has been jailed on terrorism charges; Isamuddin has not been seen since Sept 11 (M)

2002-02-03.title: School in Indonesia Urges 'Personal Jihad' In Steps of bin Laden
2002-02-03.abstract: Al Mukmim boarding school in Solo, Indonesia, champions Osama bin Laden and exhorts Muslims to follow his example; photos; map; principal Abu Bakar Baasyir denies role in terrorist activities but admits he taught 13 of people arrested in plot to blow up US Embassy in Singapore; declares US is 'terrorist nation' and says he 'rejoiced' at Sept 11 attacks, which he claims were masterminded by Americans and Israel; Indonesia has for hundreds of years taken pride in system of religious schools where poor students learned laissez-faire form of Islam; 'pesantren' were considered incubators of demoracy, and moral values; Al Mukmin, in contrast, was set up by Baasyir in 1972 as standard-bearer for Islamic state, indoctrinating boys as young as five to be foot soldiers in 'personal jihad' against US; Solo, commercial city and bastion of conservative Islam, has more than 70 Islamic groups, mostly new and radical (M)

2002-02-03.title: Italy Inching Toward Lifting Exile for Its Royal Heirs
2002-02-03.abstract: Italian Senate is poised to take what could be first steps toward allowing exiled heirs of Italy's last king to return home; youngest heir to throne, Prince Emanuele Filiberto di Savoia, has lived all his 30 years in Switzerland; says his family no longer believes promises of Italian politicians; despite his family's long campaign to be allowed back in, Emanuele Filiberto and his father, Vittorio Emanuele IV, who left at age 9, have made several angry remarks that have done nothing to hasten their return; photo (M)

2002-02-03.title: Cemetery Hunt Fails to Find American Amid Confusion
2002-02-03.abstract: Police in Karachi, Pakistan, search scores of cemeteries after receiving new communique that offers hope that Daniel Pearl, Wall Street Journal reporter kidnapped 10 days ago, is still alive; previous communique claiming Pearl is dead and parallel claim demanding ransom cast pall over headquarters of Wall Street Journal in New York; photo (M)

2002-02-03.title: Troops of U.S. And Kenya Plan Exercise
2002-02-03.abstract: More than 2000 US marines, fresh from Afghanistan, will begin three-week exercise off Kenya coast; Pentagon says exercise with Kenyan forces was planned for year and does not foreshadow imminent operation in Somalia, where Al Qaeda fighters may have fled(S)

2002-02-04.title: Waiting Out the Siege, Arafat Remains Defiant
2002-02-04.abstract: Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat is waiting out siege in Ramallah on West Bank, where he has been confined by Israeli forces for past two months since wave of suicide bombings in Israel in early December; chafes at his house arrest and maneuverings of old rival Prime Min Ariel Sharon; seethes at American antagonism, which he considers deeply unjust; Pres Bush has ignored his recent pleas for American intervention and assailed his credibility and his commitment to peace; photo (M)

2002-02-04.title: Global Forum: Sharing Viewpoints
2002-02-04.abstract: News analysis of reasons why World Economic Forum draws so many powerful people each year; gives participants chance to do business in nonthreatening setting in which they can criticize and be criticized; photo (M)

2002-02-04.title: Kidnapped Brazilian Ad Executive Is Freed in a Police Raid
2002-02-04.abstract: Police squad in Sao Paulo, Brazil, raids deserted home in capital and rescues kidnapped advertising executive Washington Olivetto, bringing end to highest-profile case in recent wave of abductions; arrests six suspects, who may be linked to guerrilla organizations in neighboring countries; rescue ends nearly two months of captivity for Olivetto, one of Latin America's leading advertising executives (M)

2002-02-04.title: Pôrto Alegre Journal; A Leftist City Makes Money to Its Own Drummer
2002-02-04.abstract: World Social Forum is held in Porto Alegre, socialist stronghold in southern Brazil to counter World Economic Forum in New York; meeting, from which World Bank and other pillars of global economic establishment, are excluded affords city leaders opportunity to swap ideas on containing global capitalism with representatives of European and American left; participants run gamut from members of Brazilian fringe groups to polished members of European parliament; maps; photo (M)

2002-02-04.title: Argentina Offers Economic Plan, Hoping to Sidestep Court
2002-02-04.abstract: Argentine government will allow peso to float freely against dollar and will ease, but not eliminate, freeze on bank accounts recently ruled unconstitutional by courts; measures are seen as attempt to undermine adverse decision of unpopular Supreme Court while reaching out to foreign creditors; Economy Min Jorge Remes Lenicov says all financial debts and assets will be converted from dollars into pesos, but at differing rates that could signal big losses for banks but make change less painful for debtors and account holders; photo (M)

2002-02-05.title: Error Possible In Attacks, Rumsfeld Says
2002-02-05.abstract: Defense Sec Donald H Rumsfeld acknowledges for first time that American troops in Afghanistan might have mistakenly killed or wounded friendly Afghans in raids on two compounds north of Kandahar in January; previously dismissed claims by Afghan villagers that American airstrikes or raids had erred; says US forces may have been fired on first, then returned fire; says incident reflects often confusing situation on ground with uncertain loyalties and imperfect intelligence posing huge challenges for American troops (M)

2002-02-05.title: World Briefing | Americas: Costa Rica: Runoff In Presidential Race
2002-02-05.abstract: Abel Pacheco and Rolando Araya, candidates of Costa Rica's two main parties, will face each other in April runoff after neither manages to secure outright victory in February 3 presidential election (S)

2002-02-05.title: Israeli's Dual Job: Chief of Defense, and Dissent
2002-02-05.abstract: Interview with Benjamin Ben-Eliezer, Israel's defense minister and newly elected leader of its labor Party--in theory most powerful left-leaning counterweight inside or outside government to Prime Min Ariel Sharon's rightist Likud; he comments on holding both jobs and clash of values it can create; photo (M)

2002-02-05.title: World Briefing | Africa: Burundi: Another Step In Peace Effort
2002-02-05.abstract: New senate, equally divided between Hutus and Tutsis, is formally installed as part of Burundi's continuing effort to end ethnic strife (S)

2002-02-05.title: 8-Year Investigation of Corruption at French Oil Company Ends
2002-02-05.abstract: Eight-year investigation into web of corruption linked to Elf Aquitaine, former state-owned French oil company, officially ends; implicated more than 40 people, including former minister Charles Pasqua who is presidential candidate; allegations date from before privatization in 1994, era when French government used company as tool of foreign policy, especially in Africa where it had huge investments; investigators say company amassed multimillion-dollar slush fund that was sometimes used to gain influence and win contracts and was siphoned off by politicians and directors (M)

2002-02-06.title: POWELL SAYS U.S. PLANS TO WORK OUT BINDING ARMS PACT
2002-02-06.abstract: Sec of State Colin L Powell tells Senate committee that United States expects to meet Russia's demand for legally binding agreement on reducing nuclear warheads, whether that takes form of treaty approved by Congress or some less formal document; leaves most details unspecified, and officials say they are still being worked out; Sen Chuck Hagel expresses misgivings about Pres Bush's linking of Iraq, Iran and North Korea in 'axis of evil,' and Powell replies that Bush was serious and not using rhetorical flourish (M)

2002-02-06.title: World Briefing | Europe: Italy: Royal Exiles Win Round 1
2002-02-06.abstract: Italian Senate votes to let exiled male heirs of Vittorio Emanuele III back into country (S)

2002-02-06.title: London Journal; Uneasy Heads in the Palace: Will Jubilee Year Flop?
2002-02-06.abstract: Celebration of Queen Elizabeth II's 50th year on British throne gets off to inauspicious start, and there are fears that it might become rerun of botched millennium celebrations; British public has become less deferential to monarchy and less appreciative of royal family since 1977, when queen had triumphant Silver Jubilee; photo (M)

2002-02-06.title: Fugitive, Returning to France, Jolts Presidential Campaign
2002-02-06.abstract: Didier Schuller is extradited to Paris, where he is expected to give testimony to support longstanding allegations of large-scale illicit funding scheme when city's mayor was Jacques Chirac, now president of France; people in Chirac's Rally for the Republic Party, accuse Socialist government of timing extradition to undermine Chirac's re-election chances; polls have only recently and only slightly turned in favor of Chirac's likely challenger, Prime Min Lionel Jospin, Socialist leader; photo (M)

2002-02-06.title: Terror Acts By Baghdad Have Waned, U.S. Aides Say
2002-02-06.abstract: US intelligence officials say CIA has no evidence that Iraq has engaged in terrorist operations against United States in nearly a decade and is also convinced that Pres Saddam Hussein has not provided chemical or biological weapons to Al Qaeda or related terrorist groups; say agency believes meeting in Prague between Mohamed Atta, leader of hijacking teams of Sept 11, and Iraqi intelligence officer does not provide sufficient evidence of Iraqi involvement; say agency's greatest concern with Iraq is its continuing development of chemical and biological weapons (M)

2002-02-07.title: French Minister Calls U.S. Policy 'Simplistic'
2002-02-07.abstract: French Foreign Min Hubert Vedrine openly criticizes Washington's approach to terrorism as 'simplistic' and scores United States for conducting foreign policy without consulting its allies; remarks seen as sign of frustration with Pres Bush's worldview as described in his State of Union address; Vedrine, in interview on France's Inter radio, suggests Europeans must speak out more and more because they face US that acts 'unilaterally' based only on its own interests; Secretary of State Colin L Powell rejects criticism, saying it is far from truth; Vedrine's comments may be harshest yet, but other European leaders are also beginning to distance themselves from Washington; even British Prime Min Tony Blair has warned against military strike on Iraq unless clear connection is found between it and Sept 11 attacks; photo (M)

2002-02-07.title: Geneva Convention to Be Applied to Captured Taliban Fighters
2002-02-07.abstract: President Bush has decided to apply the Geneva Convention to Taliban fighters, the White House announced today.

2002-02-07.title: World Briefing | Europe: France: Sentence For Big Mac Attacker
2002-02-07.abstract: France's highest appeals court upholds three-month jail term for Jose Bove for his ransacking of MacDonald's restaurant site in Millau in 1999 to protest American trade policy (S)

2002-02-07.title: Cuba, Too, Felt the Sept. 11 Shock Waves, With a More Genial Castro Offering Help
2002-02-07.abstract: Pres Fidel Castro of Cuba has muted his hostile remarks about Washington and offered to cooperate with Bush administration's war on terrorism in wake of Sept 11 attacks; within hours of attacks, he offered medical help as well as intelligence on movement of terrorists; Castro has not criticized United States decision to detain Taliban and Al Qaeda prisoners at American military base at Guantanamo Bay; his warmer tone appears to be part of effort to avoid deterioration in relations with Washington as Cuba faces its own economic crisis; photo (M)

2002-02-07.title: German Unemployment Is Growing Problem for Schröder
2002-02-07.abstract: German unemployment figures rise sharply in Jan, above psychologically important four milion total; new figure is political blow to Chancellor Gerhard Schroder, who said four years ago that if his governement could not hold number below 3.5 million by next election, which is scheduled for Sept 22, it would not deserve to be re-elected; adding to Schroder's woes is budding scandal about falsification of job-creation figures by state employment offices (M)

2002-02-08.title: Dead and Missing
2002-02-08.abstract: Officials estimate that as of yesterday, 3,067 people had died, or were missing and presumed dead, as a result of the attacks on Sept. 11, not including 19 hijackers.    AT THE WORLD TRADE CENTER  2,843 dead or missing, with 147 dead on two hijacked planes  199 missing  711 death certificates issued by medical examiner's office  1,933 death certificates issued at request of families in cases in which remains have not been identified

2002-02-08.title: Confession in 1994 Case Evokes Pearl Abduction
2002-02-08.abstract: Pakistani and American officials say Ahmed Omar Sheikh, Islamic fundamentalist, is chief suspect in abduction of American journalist Daniel Pearl; cite 45-page account of kidnapping plan that Sheikh wrote 7 years ago after being jailed for kidnapping of three Britons and an American; that plan bears striking resemblances to Pearl's kidnapping; Rhys Partridge, one of Britons held captive by Sheikh, says he displayed mixture of intelligence, irrationality and intensity that led him, Partridge, to become convinced that Sheikh was psychopath (M)

2002-02-08.title: St. Merryn Journal; Europe Decrees. British Bristle. Iceboxes Heaved.
2002-02-08.abstract: Mountains of discarded refrigerators in Britain are visible side effect of new European law that requires discarded appliances to be taken to special plants where environmentally harmful, ozone-depleting chlorofluorocarbons are extracted from insulation foam; Britain neglected to build plants, and this oversight has brought charges of incompetence from Conservative opposition and round of finger-pointing between defensive Labor government and European Union; photo (M)

2002-02-08.title: World Briefing | Europe: Ireland: Rise In Unwed Parents
2002-02-08.abstract: Economic and Social Research Institute reports one in three Irish children born in 2000 were born to unmarried parents, up from one in 20 in 1980 (S)

2002-02-08.title: Sharon Tells Bush He Expects Creation of Palestinian State
2002-02-08.abstract: Prime Min Ariel Sharon of Israel tells Pres Bush that 'Israel is committed to peace' and that he expects to see Palestinian state at end of Middle East peace process; two hold 45-minute meeting at White House, at which Bush says United States will keep up pressure on Yasir Arafat to crack down on violence; American and Israeli officials say Sharon's words about Palestinian state, made with American president at his side, are sign of his willingness to move toward negotiations with Palestinians, and reflects slight warming in his hard-line appraoch (M)

2002-02-09.title: Agency Differs With U.S. Over P.O.W.'s
2002-02-09.abstract: International Committee of Red Cross says it differs with United States on how to decide whether to grant prisoner of war status to captives in Afghanistan; says person captured in international conflict is presumed to be prisoner of war unless competent tribunal decides otherwise (S)

2002-02-09.title: North Korea Cancels Trip By 4 Experts From the U.S.
2002-02-09.abstract: korea: north korea cancels trip by 4 experts fro

2002-02-09.title: Israeli Woman Stabbed to Death in a Wave of Violence
2002-02-09.abstract: Group of Arab youths stab to death Israeli woman, 24, who was walking in park between Jewish and Arab areas of Jerusalem; police report catching four of masked Palestinians spotted running away; say one died at scene, without being shot or wounded; plan autopsy; thousands of supporters of Islamic group Hamas rally, Gaza Strip; watch performers act out rocket attack on Jewish settlement; Hamas leader Dr Abdel Aziz Rantisi speaks in first public appearance since he agreed with Palestinian Authority to submit to house arrest in Dec; deal, together with limited cease-fire set by Hamas, seems to have collapsed; Rantisi says suicide attacks will continue; Palestinian leadership again calls for cease-fire; map; photo (M)

2002-02-09.title: Afghan-Pakistan Talks
2002-02-09.abstract: Reaching back more than a decade to find common ground for their countries' future relationship, the leaders of Afghanistan and Pakistan agreed at a meeting that ''misunderstandings and misperceptions'' of the past should be left behind.    Article, Page A8.

2002-02-09.title: Key Allies Oppose Deficit Warning : Berlin Looks Set To Escape Rebuke
2002-02-09.abstract: FRANKFURT—  Germany appeared close to blocking an unprecedented censure of its ballooning deficit that would deal an election-year humiliation to Chancellor Gerhard Schroeder, officials said Friday.

2002-02-10.title: Khmer Rouge Trials Won't Be Fair, Critics Say
2002-02-10.abstract: Cambodians feel betrayed and abandoned after United Nations gives up effort to create international tribunal to try former leaders of Khmer Rouge; UN ended nearly five years of negotiations over makeup and procedures of tribunal, worn down by stubbornness of Cambodian government; this leaves government to proceed on its own with trials that both Cambodian and foreign analysts say will not meet international standards of fairness and impartiality and are likely to spare most major figures; Khmer Rouge, led by late Pol Pot, were responsible for deaths of more than million people when they held power from 1975 to 1979 (M)

2002-02-10.title: Blast Further Erodes Nigerians' Confidence
2002-02-10.abstract: Accidental explosion in arms depot in Lagos, Nigeria, comes after months of rising lawlessness and focuses anger on elected government that is widely seen as ineffective, uncaring and dangerously fragile; more than 1,000 people died, trampled on and drowned in canal as tens of thousands were fleeing city; government's response to Lagos disaster has hardened Nigerians' low opinion of their leaders; Pres Olusegun Obasanjo, in visit to military barracks near arms depot, expresses little sympathy for victims and refuses peoples' request to visit munitions dumps; when Vice Pres Atiku Abubakar visits barracks, his car is stoned and he is forced to flee; photo; map (M)

2002-02-10.title: Security Bill for Australia Is Too Broad, Critics Say
2002-02-10.abstract: SYDNEY, Australia, Feb. 9 — Australia wants firmer penalties and longer jail sentences for convicted spies and traitors, but broad legislation proposed after Sept. 11 for that purpose could also imprison public sector whistle-blowers and journalists who expose scandals, not state secrets, civil libertarians and legal experts contend.

2002-02-10.title: When Putin Says 'Exercise!' Russia Treads a Beaten Path
2002-02-10.abstract: Pres Vladimir V Putin's campaign to get Russians to exercise more fails to move average citizen, but politicians are scrambling to fulfill his will; mayor of Saransk takes his entire government on biathlon-style jaunt, and governor of St Petersburg orders his aides to prepare fitness program for all 6,000 government workers; this has provoked debate about national character, wisdom of imposing change from above and even president's commitment to democracy; critics wonder whether Putin and country are breaking Soviet mold or simply reverting to it; Izvestia columnist says 'habit to obey is in our blood'; photo (M)

2002-02-10.title: For Women in Kabul, This Test Is Welcome
2002-02-10.abstract: Hundreds of young Afghan women take exam to attend Kabul University, idea that was anathema to former Taliban rulers; university faces many problems; Dr Aziz Ahmad Rahmand, professor of Afghan history, recalling Taliban repression, says he is thrilled that new day is dawning for education in his country; photos (M)

2002-02-11.title: Britain Tries To Quell Fear Over Vaccine For Children
2002-02-11.abstract: British government struggles to quell fears about common childhood vaccine that increasing numbers of parents believe may be linked to autism, despite strong official assurances to contrary; government says so-called MMR vaccine, administered in two doses, is perfectly safe; vaccine has mostly wiped out three potentially deadly diseases, measles, mumps and rubella; Prime Min Tony Blair is under pressure from press and parents' groups to say whether his 20-month-old son, Leo, has had vaccine; Blair strongly implies that child has been or will be inoculated; growing numbers of parents decide not to allow their children to be inoculated, citing anecdotal evidence from parents of autistic children as well as several studies that suggest links between MMR and autism; meanwhile, measles has been diagnosed in 11 children in London, where only 75 percent of eligible toddlers have been immunized as opposed to 90.5 percent in United States (M)

2002-02-11.title: From the Austrian Wunderteam, a suprise
2002-02-11.abstract: SALT LAKE CITY:—  A year ago, at the World Ski Championships in Austria, an American named Daron Rahlves upstaged his hosts to win the opening men's race. On Sunday, in the first race of Ralves's home Olympics, the Austrians returned the favor.

2002-02-11.title: Rehabilitation Joins Retribution in Saudi Drug War
2002-02-11.abstract: Drug abuse in Saudi Arabia is serious enough problem that rulers are beginning to deal with it in very un-Saudi way: openly; government treats drug trafficking as criminal activity, but it has begun to regard drug, alcohol and nicotine dependence as treatable illnesses; drugs have increasingly become source of entertainment and escape in country that bans movie theaters, concert halls, discotheques and dating, and where 65 percent of population is under age of 25; country now has four drug treatment centers where patients are offered group therapy, physical therapy and biofeedback; photos (M)

2002-02-11.title: Trial of Milosevic Will Peel Layers of Balkan Guilt, Too
2002-02-11.abstract: Slobodan Milosevic, former president of Yugoslavia who goes on trial Feb 12, is first head of state ever to be tried for war crimes; is essentially accused of leading genocidal, criminal enterprise to 'cleanse' land he believed belonged to ethnic Serbs of other ethnic groups in three wars in Croatia, Kosovo and Bosnia; he proclaims his innocence, saying he wanted only to keep Yugoslavia whole; even those who loathe Milosevic worry that proceedings may look like victors' justice, and wonder whether prosecutor Carla Del Ponte can deliver evidence that draws direct line between Milosevic and bodies that have been uncovered in mass graves in Bosnia and elsewhere; trial is expected to last two years; beyond trial itself is question of what it means for people of what was once much larger Yugoslavia; photos; timeline; map (L)

2002-02-11.title: Tehran Shuts Offices of Afghan Hard-Liner as Calls to Expel Him Increase
2002-02-11.abstract: Government of Iran shutters offices in Tehran of Afghan warlord Gulbuddin Hekmatyar, indicating he might be asked to leave; Hekmatyar's criticism of interim government in Kabul, plus his veiled threats to foreign forces stationed there, are at odds with Iran's own stated policy of helping to create stable government next door; his expulsion would also serve as yet another signal to Washington of Iran's good intentions; photo (M)

2002-02-12.title: Storm in Mexico Devastates Monarch Butterfly Colonies
2002-02-12.abstract: Severe mid-January winter storm in mountains of central Mexico devastated two of country's largest monarch butterfly colonies; record 74 percent to 80 percent, or between 220 million and 270 million, butterflies died; researchers say loss is not expected to threaten species; photo; maps (M)

2002-02-12.title: Pentagon Faces More Questions on Afghan Raid, Plus C.I.A. Role
2002-02-12.abstract: The Pentagon today sought to fend off continued questions about a missile attack by a C.I.A. drone in Afghanistan, and faced a number of questions about the agency's role in the war on terrorism.

2002-02-12.title: Tribunal: How It Works
2002-02-12.abstract: Article describes workings of International Criminal Tribunal for former Yugoslavia (S)

2002-02-12.title: World Briefing | World: United Nations: U.S. Nominee
2002-02-12.abstract: United Nations Secretary Gen Kofi Annan nominates James T Morris, an American, as executive director of World Food Program; photo (S)

2002-02-12.title: Asians take a closer look at Islamic schools
2002-02-12.abstract: SINGAPORE—  Without warning, a phalanx of riot police and plainclothes officers descended on a school for about 200 young children in a lush oil palm estate in the southern Malaysian state of Johor, adjacent to Singapore. The raid, on Jan. 3, led to the closure of the Islamic school, or madrasa, which Malaysian authorities say was linked to a group of Muslim extremists.

2002-02-13.title: Russian Tycoon's Bombshell: Can It Be?
2002-02-13.abstract: Residents of 12-story apartment block on edge of industrial city of Ryazan, 130 miles southeast of Moscow, recall mysterious incident in Sept 1999, in which Russian intelligence agents planted what appeared to be huge bomb that, had it exploded, would have killed hundreds of people; official account is that it was extremely ill-considered civil defense drill, but incident occurred in wake of apartment bombings that did kill more than 300 people; Russian oligarch Boris A Berezovsky says he will show documentary film proving federal agents were involved in explosions, with Ryazan dummy bomb as evidence, but his claims are also doubted; photo (M)

2002-02-13.title: Afghan Official Says Pakistanis Helped bin Laden Evade Capture
2002-02-13.abstract: Senior Afghan official Yunus Qanooni accuses elements in Pakistan's intelligence service of helping Osama bin Laden and Mullah Mohammad Omar evade capture (S)

2002-02-13.title: Brutal Death Of Immigrant Shakes Faith Of Spaniards
2002-02-13.abstract: Coldly brutal death of Wilson Pacheco, 26-year-old illegal immigrant from Ecuador who was beaten by nightclub bouncers and thrown into Barcelona harbor in January, has many Spaniards asking whether their society is as racially tolerant as they have believed (M)

2002-02-13.title: World Briefing | World: Ban On Young Soldiers
2002-02-13.abstract: UN says global ban on child soldiers has gone into effect; treaty bars recruitment of children under 18 by armies and militias, practice that affects estimated 300,000 children (S)

2002-02-14.title: Annan Asks U.S. for More Money for AIDS Fund
2002-02-14.abstract: Secretary General Kofi Annan of United Nations meets privately with Pres Bush and members of Senate to prod them to give more for new global fund to combat AIDS epidemic; reportedly asks United States to set example for other countries by increasing international AIDS spending by 50 percent each year for next four years; Bush's budget proposal for 2003 asks for $900 million in international spending for AIDS prevention and treatment; pledge is not increase over this year and advocates and lawmakers say it is not enough (M)

2002-02-14.title: From Serb, One More Protest About His Trial
2002-02-14.abstract: Excerpts from angry exchange between Slobodan Milosevic and Richard May, presiding judge at International Criminal Tribunal for the Former Yugoslavia (M)

2002-02-14.title: Peres Offers New Peace Plan to Arafat and Sharon
2002-02-14.abstract: Israeli foreign minister Shimon Peres draws up new peace plan calling for cease-fire with Palestinians, immediate recognition of initial Palestinian state on limited turf and yearlong negotiation over what final state would look like; he negotiated new plan with speaker of Palestinian parliament, known as Abu Ala; Peres faces solitary quest to sell plan to Prime Min Ariel Sharon, who resisted his negotiating in first place, to his party, which is under new, more hawkish leadership, and to Israelis, most of whom have given up on Yasir Arafat, Palestinian leader; Sharon has criticized peace plan, but he has not yet rejected it; photo (M)

2002-02-14.title: Hunt for U.S. Reporter Leading to Dead Ends
2002-02-14.abstract: Police in Pakistan report that arrest and interrogation of Ahmed Omar Sheikh, chief suspect in kidnapping of American journalist Daniel Pearl, has produced no solid evidence on Pearl's whereabouts or whether he is still alive; Gen Perez Musharraf, in visit to Washington, says he is 'reasonably sure' that Pearl is still alive, and that he regards abduction as retaliation by Muslim extremists to his efforts to crack down on terror; it is unclear whether police found Sheikh or whether he turned himself in as part of arrangement for freeing of relatives and colleagues who have been detained in case; photo (M)

2002-02-14.title: Traudl Junge, 82, Hitler's Private Secretary
2002-02-14.abstract: Traudl Junge, private secretary to Hitler who was last surviving witness to Nazi leader's final moments in his Berlin bunker, dies at age 82 (S)

2002-02-15.title: World Briefing | Americas: Brazil: An Effort To Make Smokers Think Twice
2002-02-15.abstract: Health Ministry of Brazil orders tobacco companies to display on cigarette packs photos showing effects of smoking; Brazil has been tightening regulations on tobacco industry since 1995, banning cigarette advertisements at sporting and cultural events and forbidding smoking in enclosed areas (S)

2002-02-15.title: Italians Hope Blair Visit Helps Berlusconi's European Image
2002-02-15.abstract: Many Italians hope British Prime Min Tony Blair's upcoming visit will help end Italy's current isolation within Europe, 'reinvent' Prime Min Silvio Berlusconi and reposition Italy in new alliance with Britain and Spain on economic and European policy (M)

2002-02-15.title: Mine Wrecks Tank; 3 Israeli Soldiers Die
2002-02-15.abstract: For the first time, Palestinians destroyed an Israeli tank, planting a mine that punched through its belly and killed at least three soldiers.    The attack followed an Israeli incursion into three Gaza towns. That, in turn, was a reponse to the Palestinian firing of rockets into Israel.

2002-02-15.title: Europeans Give Bush Plan on Climate Change a Tepid Reception
2002-02-15.abstract: European Union gives tepid welcome to Pres Bush's new proposals, strictly voluntary, to address climate change (M)

2002-02-15.title: Arabs Deploy New Explosive Against Tank; 3 Israelis Die
2002-02-15.abstract: Palestinian militants destroy Israeli tank for first time, using mine underneath that kills at least three soldiers and lightly wounds fourth; tank was responding to coordinated attack on settlers' convoy in Gaza; Hamas and Fatah faction claim responsibility; Israel sees escalation to conventional warfare; Israeli raids in Gaza and West Bank and killings of several Palestinians also noted (S)

2002-02-16.title: Oh, This Shark Has Missing Teeth, Dear
2002-02-16.abstract: Efren Reyes of Philippines may be world's best pool player and biggest money winner in that sport; he spent his best pool years--between 18 and 24--in lucrative obscurity before becoming famous; he is now 47; in space of eight days last Nov he won two of biggest billiards championships in world, in Warsaw and Tokyo; photo (M)

2002-02-16.title: Banking offshore : Even for a middle roller, it can make sense
2002-02-16.abstract: Having an offshore bank account may sound exotic and racy and not quite on the upand-up, but it can be a useful — and legitimate — financial planning tool for expatriates, providing tax savings and convenience.

2002-02-16.title: Vatican to Hold Off Opening Pope Pius XII Wartime Archives
2002-02-16.abstract: Vatican announces that it will not make available documents on Pope Pius XII's controversial World War II pontificate for at least three more years; Jewish leaders and scholars express disappointment; some scholars theorize that Vatican is trying to get out in front of wave of criticism of Pius XII expected after release of new Costa Gavras film Amen, movie adaption of Rolf Hochhuth's play The Deputy, in which Pius is shown refusing to act on information about Holocaust; Pope John Paul II, only one with authority to open archives, has decided to start with release of selected documents on ties between Vatican and Germany from 1922 to 1939 (M)

2002-02-16.title: Asylum Seekers Set Fires at Detention Center in Britain
2002-02-16.abstract: Fire and riots tear through Yari's Wood, Britain's showcase holding center for asylum seekers in what police say appears to be attempted mass escape by some of its 380 inmates; six people are reported injured; 20 asylum seekers, thought to be from Eastern Europe, escape, but 10 of them are recaptured; incident seen as severe challenge to Prime Min Tony Blair's government, which has sought to accelerate deportations and gathered those it plans to expel at centers like Yari's Wood; growing influx of migrants from developing countries and Eastern Europe has inspired political duel between Labor government and opposition Conservatives, with each side accusing other of being soft on economic fugitives seeking sanctuary as political refugees; photo (M)

2002-02-16.title: Milosevic Vows a Parade of Witnesses, Including Clinton
2002-02-16.abstract: Slobodan Milosevic says he will call present and past world leaders, including former Pres Bill Clinton, to stand during his war crimes trial at Hague; says he will personally cross-examine witnesses, including those who were raped, tortured or had their families killed, purportedly by Serbs; International War Crimes Tribunal must manage defendant who seems likley to represent himself with full-bore focus during trial that may last two years; Milosevic outlines his defense during defiant speech lasting over four hours; photo (M)

2002-02-17.title: Arab Experts Fault Saudi's Idea Based on Land-for-Peace Trade
2002-02-17.abstract: Arab experts say tentative proposal from Crown Prince Abdullah of Saudi Arabia for ending Middle East violencethrough longstanding land-for-peace formula would only serve to convince Israel that inflexibility toward ending its occupation works (M)

2002-02-17.title: Awe and Unease as South Africa Stretches Out
2002-02-17.abstract: South Africa shakes off decades of apartheid-era isolation and stretches beyond its borders in powerful explosion of trade and investment that fellow Africans are watching with awe and admiration, but also unease at prospect of ceding so much control to outsiders; development underscores metamorphosis since apartheid ended in 1994 as former pariah state seems poised to dominate continent that once shunned its products and leaders; economic expansion is accompanied by growing political influence; many people praise country's championing of new partnerships among African nations they hope will reduce economic dependency on former colonial rulers, but there are also cries of outrage from politicians and businessmen who view rapid expansion as new form of colonialism; map; charts; photo (L)

2002-02-17.title: As Crime Rises, London Looks To New York For Lessons
2002-02-17.abstract: Focus on sharp increase in street crime in London, where officials say pedestrians are more likely to be mugged than in New York; visit by former New York City Mayor Rudolph Giuliani, well known for tough line against crime, has focused government's attention on problem and sent embarrassed officials scrambling for ideas on how to solve it; street crimes increased 40.3 percent in London in past year; Home Sec David Blunkett gives police officials six months to achieve steep reduction in violent street crime and two years to make heavy inroads into problem of crime in general (M)

2002-02-17.title: Library Treasures on International Loan Delight Australians
2002-02-17.abstract: Thousands unexpectedly line up outside National Library in Canberra, Australia, to view exhibition of rare books, letters, manuscripts, writers' possessions and other literary treasures borrowed from 42 collections in 24 countries; exhibit is part of celebration of library's centennial (M)

2002-02-17.title: West Bank Suicide Bombing Kills 2 Israelis and Hurts 30
2002-02-17.abstract: Suicide bomber detonates explosive package in outdoor food court of West Bank settlement of Karnei Shomron, killing himself and two Israelis and injuring 30 others; bombing follows already deadly day in West Bank and Gaza Strip; Palestinian militants are testing new tactics in stepped-up exchange of attack and retaliation and Israeli Army is searching for new ways to strike back; Israeli soldiers in Gaza kill three Palestinians in gun battle; leader of Hamas in West Bank city of Jenin dies in explosion that Palestinians say is Israeli assassination; Palestinian militants fire rockets into Israeli Army base in northern Gaza Strip, damaging building; Hamas militants fire rockets from Gaza into Israel, injuring no one but drawing new warnings of retaliation for what Israeli officials consider marked escalation in fighting; photo (M)

2002-02-18.title: Ani Pachen, Warrior Nun In Tibet Jail 21 Years, Dies
2002-02-18.abstract: Ani Pachen, celebrated as warrior nun who led her Tibetan clan in armed rebellion against Chinese invaders until she was captured and spent 21 years as prisoner, dies at 68 or 69; photo (M)

2002-02-18.title: 4 Die in Explosion at Brake Plant in Mexico
2002-02-18.abstract: An explosion at a brake-pad factory near Mexico City killed four plant workers and injured seven others, the police reported Sunday.

2002-02-18.title: Finding a Tepid Ally in the War on Terror, U.S. Presses Indonesia to Arrest 2 Clerics
2002-02-18.abstract: Bush administration, frustrated that Indonesia has not cracked down on terrorist threat, urges officials to hunt for Indonesian cleric, Riudan Isamuddin or Hambali, who is believed to be Al Qaeda point man in Southeast Asia; US believes he led plot to blow up US Embassy in Singapore; Indonesians also say they do not have enough evidence to arest second cleric, Abu Bakar Baasyir, alleged conduit of money (M)

2002-02-18.title: Where the Bodies Are Buried in Peru
2002-02-18.abstract: Special Peruvian government commission, 15 months after fall of autocratic government of Pres Alberto K Fujimori, opens window into 20 years of state-sponsored repression and rebel terror that tore country apart; Truth and Reconciliation Commission is beginning probe of killings and disappearances of thousands of people, many from province of Ayacucho; is interviewing relatives of victims and disinterring remains; will collect testimony in private and will hold public hearings, first in Latin America, where victims of violence and public officials can give their accounts; maps; photos (M)

2002-02-18.title: Canada Alters Security Policies to Ease Concerns of U.S.
2002-02-18.abstract: Canada has overhauled security policy since Sept 11 terrorist attacks, tightening coordination with US on military, intelligence and law enforcement matters, and easing traditional concerns with civil liberties; government may now intercept e-mail and more than 750 Canadian troops are engaged in Afghanistan; photo; 72-hour detentions without warrant are permitted in terrorism cases and national security budgets are increased; shift aims to reassure US that Canada is not sanctuary for terrorists exploiting liberal refugee policies and porous border, but also reflects economic shock of long border-crossing delays; coordinated policies explained (M)

2002-02-19.title: Berenson Loses Peruvian Appeal Of Her 20-Year Prison Sentence
2002-02-19.abstract: Peru's Supreme Court upholds 20-year prison term for Lori Berenson, American convicted in June 2001 of collaborating with Tupac Amaru Marxist rebel group in its war against Peruvian government; she was arrested in 1995 and sentenced to life in prison by military junta, but sentence was overturned; was retried by new three-judge panel; can seek pardon from Pres Alejandro Toledo (S)

2002-02-19.title: A Witness Says Milosevic Knew Early About Abuses in Kosovo
2002-02-19.abstract: Prosecutors at Slobodan Milosevic's war crimes trial in the Hague move swiftly to show that ousted Yugoslav leader knew or should have known of actions of his underlings, did nothing and therefore bears responsibility for any atrocities they committed; testimony of first witness former Kosovo Communist leader Mahmat Bakalli described; Bakalli says he warned Milosevic early in conflict about police abuses (M)

2002-02-19.title: In a Shift, U.S. Uses Airstrikes To Help Kabul
2002-02-19.abstract: US forces apparently open new phase in war in Afghanistan with bombing raids Afghan commanders around Kabul say were aimed at clashing militia forces rather than at Taliban or Al Qaeda; US Central Command reports warplanes dropped precision-guided bombs when 'enemy troops' attacked forces loyal to eight-week-old Kabul government near southeastern city of Khost; bombing raids place US for first time in position of using American air power in defense of government of Hamid Karzai, pro-American leader of new administration; US policy since fall of Taliban has been to offer strong political and economic support to Karzai government, which recently announced it would request American air strikes if needed to end clashes between armed factions that control much of Afghanistan outside Kabul; map (M)

2002-02-19.title: Suit to Be Filed on Behalf Of Three Detainees in Cuba
2002-02-19.abstract: Families of 26-year-old Australian man and a pair of young British citizens captured with fighters for Al Qaeda and Taliban in Afghanistan and flown to American military base in Cuba will file suit in federal court, Washington, DC, demanding their release; lawyers say clients David Hicks, Asif Iqbal and Shafiq Rasul are not terrorists and that their detention is fundamental violation of due process guarantees of Constitution; photo (M)

2002-02-19.title: Bush Affirms U.S. Role in Asia in New 'Pacific Century'
2002-02-19.abstract: Pres Bush strongly reaffirms role of American military in Asia during speech to Japanese Parliament; declares next 100 years will be 'Pacific Century;' says US will stop aggression against South Korea, show American power and purpose in support of Philippines, maintain its commitments to Taiwan and press forward with missile defense system; powerfully endorses economic reforms of Prime Min Junichiro Koizumi; photo (M)

2002-02-20.title: In Paris, Read All About It if You Can
2002-02-20.abstract: Efforts by Swedish newspaper group, Metro International, to distribute free newspapers in France is met by violence from members of printing trades chapters of General Workers Confederation, union alliance allied with Communist Party; vendors have been assaulted, printing plants vandalized, trucks attacked and stacks of newspapers dumped into gutters; union officials want government to halt all distribution of free papers until it can study effects on economy (M)

2002-02-20.title: Hundreds Killed in Train Fire in Egypt
2002-02-20.abstract: A blazing train jammed with holiday travelers sped through Egypt today, killing more than 370 passengers.

2002-02-20.title: Use of Film By Milosevic Draws Protest
2002-02-20.abstract: George Bogdanich, American filmmaker, protests unauthorized use of part of his documentary, Yugoslavia: The Avoidable War, by former Serbian leader Slobodan Milosevic at his war crimes trial in the Hague; film examines responsibility for breakup of Yugoslavia, and is particularly critical of Western support for armed separatists in Slovenia and Croatia (S)

2002-02-20.title: Office Promoting U.S.
2002-02-20.abstract: In a plan approved by President Bush, a temporary wartime effort to shape information and coordinate messages will be expanded into a permanent office of global diplomacy, intended to spread a positive image of the United States.       Article, Page A11.

2002-02-20.title: Mideast Death Toll Rapidly Rises As Violent Recriminations Spiral
2002-02-20.abstract: Palestinian gunmen stage audacious attack at West Bank outpost killing six Israeli soldiers and escaping, stunning army already reeling from deaths of seven soldiers since February 14; attack is most lethal against Israeli soldiers in West Bank and Gaza in more than 16 months of fighting; follows dizzying 24 hours of shooting, shelling, bombing and rocket attacks that have claimed lives of at least 16 people; attack on checkpoint at Ein Ariq west of Ramallah described; Israeli forces stage reprisal; attack Yasir Arafat's official compound in Gaza City, killing at least four members of his elite guard; photos (M)

2002-02-21.title: Text of Statement From The Wall Street Journal
2002-02-21.abstract: Following is the statement by Peter R. Kann, publisher of The Wall Street Journal, and Paul E. Steiger, managing editor of The Wall Street Journal on the death of reporter Daniel Pearl:.

2002-02-21.title: China Is Treated More Gently Than North Korea for Same Sin
2002-02-21.abstract: Pres Bush is told by American intelligence agencies that China has continued to supply missile technology to Iran and Pakistan, two countries that are also North Korea's primary missile customers; Bush has repeatedly called North Korea 'evil,' citing its missile sales, but aides say Bush's tone about China's continued sales of missile parts and technology will be more muted; more than year after taking office, Bush administration remains deeply divided about how to deal with China--as economic and counterterrorism partner or as strategic competitor (M)

2002-02-21.title: Rumsfeld Defends Raid in Which 12 Were Killed
2002-02-21.abstract: People killed last month in a raid in Afghanistan were not Taliban or Al Qaeda members, the defense secretary said today.

2002-02-21.title: Milosevic Cannily Cross-Examines Kosovo Massacre Survivor
2002-02-21.abstract: Slobodan Milosevic, acting as his own lawyer at his war crimes trial, cross-examines Agim Zeqiri, who says Serb troops massacred his wife and five children in Kosovo in 1999; Zeqiri testifies with his back turned to Milosevic; denies under questioning that Kosovo Liberation Army, armed Albanian guerrilla group, had been in his small village on last day he saw his family; insists that only Yugoslav forces were there, burning down houses and shooting civilians; Milosevic does not ask questions disputing that civilians were killed; rather, he implies that attack was justified by presence of Kosovo guerrillas, which Zeqiri denies over and over again (M)

2002-02-22.title: Behind-the-Scenes Clash Led Bush to Reverse Himself on Applying Geneva Conventions
2002-02-22.abstract: President Bush's decision this month to reverse himself and apply the Geneva Conventions to the Afghan war came after the Pentagon and State Department lined up against the administration's top lawyers, senior administration officials now say.    Senior officials also disclosed for the first time that NATO allies were so concerned with Mr. Bush's initial decision to reject the conventions that Britain and France warned they might not turn over Taliban and Al Qaeda fighters captured by their troops in Afghanistan unless Mr. Bush pledged to honor the treaties.

2002-02-22.title: North Korea Rejects Bush's Plea for Dialogue
2002-02-22.abstract: North Korea today rejected President Bush's plea for dialogue during his visit last week.

2002-02-22.title: Colombia Attacks Rebel Zone as Leader's Patience Snaps
2002-02-22.abstract: Colombian Air Force planes bombed rebel camps and clandestine airstrips today as thousands of troops prepared to retake a huge rebel-held zone in an offensive that may well  mark the end of a tortuous three-year peace effort.    The operation began just hours after President Andrés Pastrana, in an emotional nationwide address on Wednesday night, angrily broke off talks while accusing the rebels of hijacking a domestic airliner in order to kidnap a senior senator who was on board. The president said the Marxist guerrilla group, the Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia, was guilty of ''extreme wickedness and cruelty,'' demonstrating that his legendary patience with the rebels had been spent.

2002-02-22.title: World Briefing | Asia: Nepal: State Of Emergency Extended
2002-02-22.abstract: Nepal's Parliament votes to extend state of emergency imposed in November to battle Maoist rebellion that has spread across nation (S)

2002-02-22.title: World Briefing | Europe: Germany: Nazi Loses Court Appeal
2002-02-22.abstract: Court in Karlsruhe, Germany, rejects appeal by former SS officer Anton Malloth, who is serving life sentence for role in atrocities at Theresienstadt concentration camp during World War II (S)

2002-02-23.title: Briefly Noted; CRASH VICTIMS
2002-02-23.abstract: Bodies of three American servicemen killed in crash of Army Special Operations helicopter off Philippine coast arrive in Okinawa, Japan, as rescue crews search for other seven (S)

2002-02-23.title: World Briefing | Asia: South Korea: More Defectors From North
2002-02-23.abstract: South Korean intelligence service reports 20 more defectors from North Korea, bringing total this year to 74; total of 583 Koreans defected last year (S)

2002-02-23.title: Truce Accord In Sri Lanka Could Herald Peace Talks
2002-02-23.abstract: Prime Min Ranil Wickremesinghe of Sri Lanka signs formal cease-fire agreement with Tamil leader Velupillai Prabhakaran that is expected to lead to first peace talks between warring parties in seven years; truce brings promise of respite from 18-year-old ethnic conflict that has claimed more than 60,000 lives; Norway, which brokered deal, will lead international mission to monitor cease-fire on ground; Prime Min Jan Petersen of Norway hails accord as bold step toward peace, but also seeks to discourage unrealistic hopes of quick resolution to one of world's most intractable civil wars (M)

2002-02-23.title: Suspect Says Reporter Was Slain In January as Part of Wider Plot
2002-02-23.abstract: Chief suspect in Daniel Pearl case, Ahmed Omar Sheikh, tells investigators that Pearl was killed in late Jan as part of plot that was to have included attack on United States Consulate in Karachi; Sheikh's claims are among clues being weighed by American and Pakistani authorities still pursuing others thought to be involved in Pearl's killing; his gruesome beheading was recorded on videotape made available to American investigators in Karachi by Pakistani journalist who covers Islamic militants; American officials praise Pakistan for its aggressive pursuit of Pearl's killers, and Pres Pervez Musharraf vows that killing would not deter him and his government from acting against terrorists; Pearl was reporter for Wall Street Journal; photo (M)

2002-02-24.title: Soldier's Burial As a Non-Jew Adds to the List Of Israel's Pain
2002-02-24.abstract: Sgt Michael Oxman, Israeli soldier killed with five comrades when Palestinian gunmen attacked West Bank outpost, is buried with full honors in non-Jewish part of military cemetery; Ukrainian immigrant considered himself Jew, like his father, but does not qualify as such under Jewish law because his mother is not Jewish (M)

2002-02-24.title: Nigeria Grows Disenchanted With Democracy
2002-02-24.abstract: Nigerians are growing disenchanted with democracy as government's positive steps are being eclipsed by unmet promises, uncontrolled violence and string of disasters; spasms of violence are expected to grow more frequent as politicians position themselves for 2003 presidential vote; over 10,000 people are believed to have been killed in ethnic and religious clashes since military rule ended in 1999; photos (M)

2002-02-24.title: Sydney De Haan, 83, Creator Of the Budget Package Tour
Damage Control
2002-02-24.abstract: Analysis of Pres Bush's Asian trip as damage control mission aimed at reassuring leaders of Japan, South Korea and China shaken by his 'axis of evil' speech that he is more interested in peace than war with Iraq, Iran and North Korea; national security adviser Condoleezza Rice hold Bush's strategy is to define problem bluntly and then follow up with diplomacy and restraint; he spent most of his time shoring up Asian alliances he needs in next phase of anti-terrorism drive (M)

2002-02-24.title: Warlord Web Thwarts Terror Fight in Yemen
2002-02-24.abstract: Yemeni Pres Ali Abdullah Saleh, although eager to prove his antiterrorist credentials to United States, is unable to produce two terrorism suspects US seeks without cooperation of tribal sheiks over whom his weak central government has little power; most of these warlords say they are on president's side and will hand over any terrorism suspects, but deny having seen them; Sheik Muhammad al Shayef comments, interview; photo (M)

2002-02-25.title: At U.N., Debating Who Gets the Right to Debate Human Rights
2002-02-25.abstract: Western regional group of countries on United Nations Human Rights Commission seeks way to avoid repeat of embarrassing surprise 10 months ago, when United States lost its seat on panel; proposed formula would allot each Western state fixed number of years on commission over next 20 years; question now is how many years should be allotted to each country; everyone agrees US should have most, but that is where consensus ends; Amb Thorsteinn Ingolfsson of Iceland has unenviable task of rotating 20 seats among 29 nations for 20 years, based on size, merit and history of countries (M)

2002-02-25.title: Saudi Proposals Bring a Bit of Hope to a Despairing Israel
2002-02-25.abstract: A report that the Saudi ruler is prepared to normalize relations with Israel if it makes peace with the Palestinians has brought a glimmer of hope to the land.

2002-02-25.title: U.S., in Pakistan Talks, Seeking Transfer of Suspect in Pearl Case
2002-02-25.abstract: Officials have begun talks on arrangements that could lead to the extradition of Ahmed Omar Sheikh, the chief suspect in the murder of Daniel Pearl.

2002-02-25.title: Suspect Linked to Regional Militants
2002-02-25.abstract: Amjed Hussain Farooqi, top fugitive being sought in Daniel Pearl case, is identified and described by Pakistani officials as man closely linked to web of Muslim militants in Afghanistan and Kashmir, reflecting view of Pakistani authorities that Pearl's killing was much more than isolated act; Farooqi is said to be former bodyguard for founder of banned group Army of Muhammad; Farooqi is suspected of picking up Pearl in Karachi and taking him to his death (M)

2002-02-25.title: Rumsfeld Says He May Drop New Office Of Influence
2002-02-25.abstract: Defense Sec Donald H Rumsfeld says Pentagon may eliminate new office intended to influence public opinion and policy makers overseas; proposals from Office of Strategic Influence have caused uproar in Congress and elsewhere in government; Rumsfeld reiterates earlier assurance that military would not be permitted to tell lies to promote American policies or views, but says disclosures about new office's potential activities may have doomed its credibility (M)

2002-02-26.title: Manzanillo Journal; In Corn's Cradle, U.S. Imports Bury Family Farms
2002-02-26.abstract: Corn exports from United States to Mexico have risen sharply in last decade because of North American Free Trade Agreement, and this development may doom growing of corn as way of life for family farmers in Manzanillo, Mex; in US, corn growers receive billions of dollars yearly in subsidies from Congress, much of it going to huge agribusiness operations; that policy fuels huge surpluses and pushes corn prices down; in Mexico, Nafta did away with many traditional subsidies and generous price supports; some contend it is doing away with small farmers; about 90 percent of Mexico's corn farmers work fields of five acres or less; their survival instincts are driving them farther and farther up Mexico's mountainsides as they strive to grow enough to get by; Mexican legislators who want Mexico to protect its corn have no luck swaying Pres Vicente Fox; photo; graph (M)

2002-02-26.title: Special Forces' Sweltering Paradise
2002-02-26.abstract: Over last two weeks 160 American Special Forces soldiers have arrived in Basilan, small island in Philippines, as part of 'military training exercise' to help Philippine Army track down and defeat Abu Sayyaf, terrorist organization that specializes in kidnapping; group is currently holding American missionaries Martin and Gracia Burnham and Filipino nurse Ediborah Yap; photo (M)

2002-02-26.title: Zimbabwe Candidate Charged With Treason
2002-02-26.abstract: Morgan Tsvangirai, who is running against Pres Robert Mugabe of Zimbabwe in upcoming election, is charged with treason in what authorities describe as plot to assassinate Mugabe; Tsvangirai is questioned and released by police; he denies accusations and his election coordinator claims charge constitutes government effort to sully his reputation before election, which is scheduled for Mar 9 and 10; incident highlights rising tensions as country braces for its most hotly contested presidential election; Mugabe, 78, has run Zimbabwe since white rule ended in 1980; opposition backers say his supporters use political disinformation and violence against them; groups of election observers from two southern African groups are stoned by rowdy supporters of Mugabe; photo (M)

2002-02-26.title: Buoyed by Saudi Peace Proposal, Israelis and Arabs Resume Talks
2002-02-26.abstract: American-brokered security talks resumed between Israelis and Palestinians in Tel Aviv today against a backdrop of swelling hopes raised by a Saudi initiative.

2002-02-27.title: BUSH WELCOMES SAUDI'S PROPOSAL ON MIDEAST PEACE
2002-02-27.abstract: Pres Bush, thrusting himself into Mideast peace effort, calls Saudi Crown Prince Abdullah and praises his proposal for Israel to withdraw completely from West Bank and Gaza in return for full normalization of relations with all Arab countries; others in administration are less enthusiastic, undertain how Prime Min Ariel Sharon will view long-term plan at time of intensifying violence; Javier Solana of European Union reports Sharon says idea is interesting; also discusses concept with Yasir Arafat, who sends team to Tel Aviv to discuss curbing violence; some Israeli ministers are heartened by what seems to be first concrete peace signal in 17 months of unmitigated violence (M)

2002-02-27.title: Czech Decrees, Dating to 40's, Divide Europe
2002-02-27.abstract: Hungarian Prime Min Viktor Orban calls for repeal of decrees issued by Edvard Benes, Czechoslovakia's postwar president, allowing for expulsion of millions of Germans and Hungarians from Czechoslovakia; Orban says repeal should be condition of European Union membership for Czech Republic and Slovakia, but demand infuriates two countries and puts pressure on partnership with Hungary and Poland (M)

2002-02-27.title: Milosevic Grills Witnesses Too Harshly, His Prosecutors Complain
2002-02-27.abstract: Prosecutors at war crimes trial at The Hague move to clamp down on Slobodan Milosevic's lengthy cross-examinations, saying grillings could deter future witnesses from testifying against him (S)

2002-02-27.title: U.S. MAY SEND G.I.'S TO EX-SOVIET AREA
2002-02-27.abstract: US considers sending 'sizable' number of Special Operations forces to former Soviet republic of Georgia to help train its military in counterterrorism tactics; aim is to prepare Georgian troops to combat foreign fighters who are operating in mountainous region and may have links to Osama bin Laden's network; new front in war against terrorism would build on operations in Afghanistan and involve 100-200 Special Operations forces who would not take part in direct combat but would defend themselves against attack (M)

2002-02-27.title: In Zimbabwe, Mugabe Runs Against an Old Rival: Colonialism
2002-02-27.abstract: Zimbabwe Pres Robert Mugabe presides at campaign rally of supporters and assails opponent Morgan Tsvangirai as front man for whites; photo (M)

2002-02-28.title: Thai Government Orders Expulsion of 2 Foreign Correspondents
2002-02-28.abstract: Thailand, one of freest and most open societies in Asia, issues rare expulsion order against two foreign correspondents, arousing cries of dismay and anger from local and foreign press; action is taken against Far Eastern Economic Review correspondents Shawn Crispin and Rodney Tasker; magazine denies any wrongdoing and appeals order; expulsion order cites brief article in magazine referring to frictions between Prime Min Thaksin Shinawatra and royal palace (M)

2002-02-28.title: World Briefing | United Nations: Annan Vows Action On Sex Abuses
2002-02-28.abstract: United Nations officials investigate claims of sexual abuse of young girls in refugee camps in West Africa; Secretary Gen Kofi Annan vows action will be taken against violators (S)

2002-02-28.title: Arafat Says Saudi Plan Needs U.S. Backing
2002-02-28.abstract: Calling it "very strong," Yasir Arafat said today that a Saudi Arabian peace proposal's chances for success depended on immediate American support.

2002-02-28.title: But ex-president's past looms large : Giscard's new role at heart of Europe
2002-02-28.abstract: BRUSSELS:—  When he presides over the first session of the "Convention on the Future of Europe" on Thursday, Valery Giscard d'Estaing will return to the center of the European political stage after 20 years in the wings.

2002-02-28.title: In Caucasus Gorge, a Haven for Muslim Militants
2002-02-28.abstract: Dozens of Afghan and Arab fighters are thought to be hiding in Pankisi Gorge, 40-mile-long slash of rock and forest in Caucasus Mountains of Georgia, possibly plotting terrorist strikes in Russia, Europe and United States; Russian and American officials want to bring fight against global terrorism to that area, but Pres Eduard Shevardnadze's government is reluctant to act while estimated 8,000 Chechen refugees and 1,500 Chechen rebels are also taking shelter there; fear is that any military operation in gorge would endanger civilians and risk bringing Russia's war in Chechnya to Georgia; area has long served as no man's land of crime, drug trafficking and hostage taking on border between Georgia and Russia; Russia has long pressed Georgia to crush Chechen rebels on its territory, or at least drive them back into Chechnya; Georgia wants United States to provide military hardware before Georgian troops are sent against hardened fighters of gorge; US and Russia are reluctant to provide such heavy weapons, concerned that they would not be used in gorge but to inflame conflicts in breakaway regions of Abkhazia and South Ossetia; photos; maps (L)
